sections.title: The rule — verb matches subject
sections.content:
The **verb** must match the **subject** in number. The danger zone is the **present simple third person singular** (he / she / it / one person or thing): it takes **-s**.
For more on *do/does* and *have/has* themselves, see [[topic:auxiliary_verbs|Auxiliary Verbs]].

sections.title: Don't be fooled by words in between
sections.content: The verb agrees with the **subject**, not with a noun that happens to sit next to the verb. Find the real subject first — phrases like *of the items* or *in the room* are not it.

items.bad: The list of items are on the table.
items.good: The list of items is on the table.

items.bad: One of the students were late.
items.good: One of the students was late.
The people in the room are waiting.

sections.title: Compound subjects
sections.content: When the subject has more than one part, the joining word decides the verb:

diagrams.title: Agreement with compound subjects
rows: Neither the teacher nor the students **were** there.
Each of us **has** a copy. Every student **takes** the test.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: he/she/it takes has (not have). He has been ill.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: she + present simple = doesn't like (do → does; like stays without -s after does).

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The list of items are on the table.
options.b: The list of items were on the table.
options.c: The list of items be on the table.
options.d: The list of items is on the table.

questions.explanation: Subject is 'the list' (singular), not 'items'. The list is on the table.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Each of the students has a book.
options.b: Each of the students have a book.
options.c: Each of the students having a book.
options.d: Each of the students are having a book.

questions.explanation: Each is singular, so the verb is singular: each has a book.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Two subjects with 'and' = plural. Tom and Anna are late.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: he has (not have).

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: she doesn't (do → does).

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: The book on the shelves are old.
options.b: The books on the shelves is old.
options.c: The book on the shelves is old.
options.d: The book on the shelf are old.

questions.explanation: Subject is 'the book' (singular). The book is old.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: He go to work by bus.
options.b: He gone to work by bus.
options.c: He going to work by bus.
options.d: He goes to work by bus.

questions.explanation: Third person singular present simple: he goes (add -es).

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Neither the teacher nor the students was there.
options.b: Neither the teachers nor the student were there.
options.c: Neither the teacher nor the students were there.
options.d: Neither the teacher or the students were there.

questions.explanation: With nor, verb agrees with the nearest subject (students = plural). Were.

options.a: One of the windows is broken.
options.b: One of the windows are broken.
options.c: One of the window is broken.
options.d: One of the windows were broken.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: it doesn't (do → does).

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Third person singular: my brother = he → has.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: News is uncountable and takes a singular verb: the news was/is bad.
